paper_title: Subgroup Analysis and Study Design of 5-Grass Pollen Tablet Treatment

methods.description: Analysis of study participants divided into age groups to assess the impact of 5-grass pollen tablet treatment on asthma, polyallergy, and RC scores.

steps.description: The majority of study participants were aged ≥ 18 years (n = 1020), with 201 aged 12-17 years and 248 aged 4-11 years.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze participant age distribution

steps.description: Asthma was present in 37.1% of the adults, 41.0% of the adolescents and 40.6% of the children.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate asthma prevalence by age group

steps.description: The percentage of polyallergic patients was 68.4% among adults, 77.8% among adolescents and 67.1% among children.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate polyallergy prevalence by age group

steps.description: Mean second-season RC score improvements versus the season preceding AIT were similar in the three age groups (all P<0.001; no significant differences in improvement between age groups).
steps.searchable_summary: compare RC score improvements by age group

steps.description: 82.4% of adults needed symptomatic medication during the pollen season preceding treatment with the 5-grass pollen tablet, compared with 84.6% of adolescents and 88.3% of children.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze medication use by age group

steps.description: The percentage of patients evaluating tolerability at V8 as 'good' to 'very good' was high in all three age groups.
steps.searchable_summary: evaluate treatment tolerability by age group

steps.description: There was a significant difference in the incidence of ADRs between children and adults and adolescents and adults, but not between children and adolescents.
steps.searchable_summary: compare ADR incidence by age group

methods.description: Analysis of patients with and without asthma to assess the impact of 5-grass pollen tablet treatment on asthma symptoms, medication use, and RC scores.

steps.description: More than a third of the overall study population had asthma in the season preceding 5-grass pollen tablet treatment.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze asthma prevalence in study population

steps.description: A significantly higher percentage of patients with asthma were polyallergic compared to patients without asthma.
steps.searchable_summary: compare polyallergy prevalence in asthma vs non-asthma patients

steps.description: The mean RC score during the season preceding 5-grass pollen tablet treatment among patients with asthma was lower compared with individuals without asthma.
steps.searchable_summary: compare RC scores in asthma vs non-asthma patients

steps.description: The frequency and severity of asthma symptoms decreased between the season preceding treatment and the second year.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze asthma symptom reduction

steps.description: During the pollen season preceding treatment, 90.0% of individuals with asthma took symptomatic medication for rhinoconjunctivitis symptoms.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze medication use in asthma patients

steps.description: Treatment tolerability was rated as 'good' to 'very good' by 97.0% of individuals with asthma.
steps.searchable_summary: evaluate treatment tolerability in asthma patients

methods.description: Analysis of polyallergic and monoallergic patients to assess the impact of 5-grass pollen tablet treatment on allergy symptoms, medication use, and RC scores.

steps.description: There were more than twice as many polyallergic as monoallergic participants.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze polyallergy vs monoallergy prevalence

steps.description: Asthma was significantly more frequent among polyallergic than monoallergic patients.
steps.searchable_summary: compare asthma prevalence in polyallergic vs monoallergic patients

steps.description: Polyallergic patients had a higher mean RC score in the season preceding treatment than monoallergic patients.
steps.searchable_summary: compare RC scores in polyallergic vs monoallergic patients

steps.description: The mean RC score showed significant improvement in response to the 5-grass pollen tablet among both polyallergic and monoallergic patients.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze RC score improvement in polyallergic vs monoallergic patients

steps.description: 84.0% of polyallergic patients needed symptomatic medication during the pollen season preceding treatment.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze medication use in polyallergic patients

steps.description: The patients of both treatment groups evaluated tolerability at V8 as 'good' to 'very good' in almost 100% of the cases.
steps.searchable_summary: evaluate treatment tolerability in polyallergic vs monoallergic patients

methods.description: Design and protocol of a prospective, open-label, uncontrolled, multicenter, non-interventional study to evaluate the effectiveness and safety of the 5-grass pollen tablet.

steps.description: This was a prospective, open-label, uncontrolled, multicenter, non-interventional study performed in Germany between September 2010 and November 2012.
steps.searchable_summary: describe study design and duration

steps.description: The study protocol was approved by the Medical Ethics Committee of the Faculty of Medicine, University of Cologne.
steps.searchable_summary: obtain ethics approval for study

steps.description: During each visit investigators filled in a case report form (CRF) for each patient.
steps.searchable_summary: document patient data in CRF

steps.description: Demographic data, history of AIT, allergy status, concomitant immunotherapy, comorbidities and start of therapy were documented at V1.
steps.searchable_summary: document baseline patient data

steps.description: Symptom severity was measured using rating scales as described by Sieber et al.
steps.searchable_summary: measure symptom severity using rating scales

steps.description: For safety evaluation, any occurrence of adverse events was documented and classified according to the Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities (MedDRA).
steps.searchable_summary: document and classify adverse events
